Hello,

Yes of course you can :) 

To start, you first need to store your access/refresh tokens after you do an `EsiSecurity.auth(code)` or `EsiSecurity.refresh()` _(You can also access it from your security attribute `access_token` and `refresh_token`)_

Once you have this, from pycrest, you have 2 solutions: 

1. Create an EVE object then use the `temptoken_authorize()` method to get your authed pycrest handler [[See API](https://github.com/pycrest/PyCrest/blob/master/pycrest/eve.py#L277)

2. You can directly instanciate an `AuthedConnection` and use it normally
```python
from pycrest import AuthedConnection

authed_pycrest = AuthedConnection(
    {
        'access_token': access_token_from_esipy,
        'refresh_token': refresh_token_from_esipy,
        'expires_in': expires_in_from_esipy
    },
    'https://crest-tq.eveonline.com',
    'https://login.eveonline.com/oauth',
    'YOUR_CLIENT_ID',
    'YOUR_SECRET_KEY',
    # cache=None, or whatever you want, if you don't know, don't include it and leave default
)
```

And then you can use it like you'd use pycrest without esipy. Just be aware that, since PyCrest auto refreshes the token, you may lose the new token if you save them somewhere. So if you save them / if you care of it, you have to manually check and update before using pycrest. 

-----

In the same way, in case someone might want to know that, you can do the same with XML API using tokens, using a lib like [evelink](https://github.com/eve-val/evelink). 
[Little example with something I do already](https://github.com/Kyria/LazyBlacksmith/blob/master/lazyblacksmith/tasks/corporation/blueprints.py#L72-L73)

> This looks like a good tutorial

Thanks for the link. There are pros and cons:

- The old Arduino based tutorial shows everything on one page whereas the Pi tutorial spreads the content over multiple pages
- The Pi tutorial "requires" some strange Adafruit breakout adapter. That might confuse users. What do you think?

Regarding this issue:

> I will adjust the pins table in the readme and add a column for the 16x2, having to look it up on external sites isn't ideal.

I disagree for the following reason:

Not all modules have the same pinout. If you buy a Char LCD on Aliexpress and blindly trust that it has the same pinout, you might fry your LCD (and maybe also your Pi?). It is important to always check the datasheet provided by the LCD manufacturer.

The Adafruit Arduino wiring tutorial mentions this and also suggests a way to calculate a resistor value, if one is needed. Therefore I think it's better if we don't provide too much information, otherwise users will rely on it and some will be disappointed when it does not work or breaks things.

Opinions?

I think trying to come up with a universal API that targets Curio/Trio at a low level (such as syscalls) leads to the same kind of trap that asyncio fell into.  Specifically, the notion that it could be a universal interface for implementing event loops and that different event-loop libraries could be built on top of asyncio.

For me, the interesting API would be at a higher level.  For example, is there a standardized API for programming sockets with async?  If so, is that API supported by asyncio, curio, trio?  If so, then perhaps you'd be able to program an application using that API without really worrying about the underlying implementation details about the library that's running it.   Focusing the API at a higher level also allows one to deal with platform specific differences such as Unix/Windows.  There is also past precedent for focusing on high-level APIs in other contexts. For example, WSGI or the Database Protocol.

I talk about this idea quite a lot in my PyOhio talk from 2016.  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E-1Y4kSsAFc

同步验签比较麻烦，因为要保证原先 `response` 中的 `key-value` 顺序。我是用下面的方式先将 `json` 字符串提取成有序字典，从而保持原先的顺序不乱：
```
def load_from_json_text(text, keep_order=True):
    """
    加载 json 格式的文本
    """
    from json import loads
    from collections import OrderedDict

    if keep_order is True:
        return loads(text, object_pairs_hook=OrderedDict)
    else:
        return loads(text)
```

然后将 `xxx_response` 提取出来（仍然是有序字典）后再转回 `json` 字符串，参见下面的函数。在重新转成 json 字符串时，要把 `,`, `:` 两边空格去掉，才能和原先的字符串一致。：
```
def to_json_text(data=None, sort_keys=True):
    """
    用于将字典转换成有序的字符串 JSON

    :param data: 字典数据
    :param sort_keys: 是否排序
    :return:
    """
    from json import dumps
    assert isinstance(data, dict)

    return dumps(data, ensure_ascii=False, sort_keys=sort_keys, separators=(',', ':'))
```

总的来说，也挺麻烦，不过多多借助了 `json` 模块。

I use the conda environment in the [modeling package](https://github.com/stadelmanma/netl-AP_MAP_FLOW) I've created to make it more cross-platform friendly. I have a small unit conversion wrapper built in that now uses pint and it's just easier to install everything from a single source, especially for non-technical users. 

I might eventually host the modeling package on conda-forge as well just to simplify things even further (especially on the Windows side).

I'd add that you can call `to_root_units()` if the desired behavior is to get the order of magnitude *independent* of "prefixed" units. So, for example, 30 m yields the same result as 0.03 km by doing the following:
```python
>>> import math
>>> q = 0.03 * ureg.kilometer
>>> q_ = q.to_root_units()
>>> '10^{{{0}}} {1.units}'.format(int(math.log10(q_.magnitude)), q_)
'10^{1} meter'
```

Longer term (after this is merged) I'd like to refactor the code a bit. It isn't your fault, but the way you had to write this to fit into the existing code is awkward. For example, `cdscan` has different meanings when `noise` is present or not. We could still calculate `cdscan` as the unweighted convolution, even when `noise` is present. Or we could just get rid of all the convolved statistics entirely and refactor the code a bit to just refer to calculating detection thresholds.

It would also be nice to find a way to avoid the x-macros for the convolve and matched filter functions. We could do this by allocating a bigger buffer of `double` that would hold multiple image and noise lines used in the convolution. E.g., for a 3x3 convolution kernel, the buffers would be 3 lines big. Then the convolution functions would only have to be defined for doubles. What version of Zenpy are you running? Anyway, I think I know what is happening here. Basically, when you create `this_ticket`, you get the state of the ticket as it was in that instant. Later, you modify the tags, however the `this_ticket` object holds the old state (ie, with all the tags). Finally, you add a comment to `this_ticket` and update it, along with the original tags. This has the effect of reverting your previous changes to the tags.

You have two options. Pull the ticket afresh after modifying the tags:

```python
this_ticket = zenpy_client.tickets(id=ticket_id)
tags_list = this_ticket.tags

if 'customerresponded' not in tags_list and '3fields' not in tags_list:
    zenpy_client.tickets.add_tags(id=ticket_id, tags=['qquote','dontnotify'])
    zenpy_client.tickets.delete_tags(id=ticket_id, tags=['followupquote','quotesent','moreinfo','other'])
    # Pull the new state of the ticket
    this_ticket = zenpy_client.tickets(id=ticket_id)

this_ticket.description = admin_id
this_ticket.status = 'open'
this_ticket.comment = Comment(body='Comment!',public=True)
zenpy_client.tickets.update(this_ticket)

zenpy_client.tickets.delete_tags(id=ticket_id, tags='dontnotify')
```

Or just work with the Ticket.tags list directly:
```python
this_ticket = zenpy_client.tickets(id=ticket_id)
if 'customerresponded' not in this_ticket.tags and '3fields' not in this_ticket.tags:
    this_ticket.tags = ['qquote']

this_ticket.description = admin_id
this_ticket.status = 'open'
this_ticket.comment = Comment(body='Comment!', public=True)
zenpy_client.tickets.update(this_ticket)
```

Hope this helps.

Hi, by default the pyasn1 library is used only for building messages to send to an LDAP server. When decoding messages from the LDAP server an internal ASN.1 decoder is used. I don't think I will switch to another asn1 library for now.

ì

It is a prototype, a proof-of-concept.  It is not intended to be directly integrated, although I think that is a conversation worth having as the world certainly would be better with one complete ldap library rather than multiple reimplementations.
As for asn1crypto, again, let me remind you this is a proof-of-concept.  I spent twenty minutes working on adding the missing message types to ldap3's encoding/decoding system and still didn't have it working.  Which for a prototype was much too long - the whole of this library took me 2 straight hours from start to finish.  So I flipped over to an admittedly imperfect asn.1 library, but doing so meant only 10 minutes of time from pip install to working server & client.
In large, I agree a custom DER encoding is the right choice.  pyasn1 is unpleasant and bog-slow.  And even if it were wonderful, a generic encoding/decoding library is an inappropriate burden for LDAP message exchange.
The only interesting places in the code are protocol.py, server.py & client.py.  I think most of your interest might be in the two starttls() methods.  SSLProtocol is part of the asyncio API - and it does all the real work.
Now what I did that's not completely above-board, is directly rerouting the linkage between the transport and protocol.  However, those parts of the transport and protocol code in asyncio are pretty stable.  And there are on-going discussions to provide a supported API for "wrapping sockets" - though the abstractions used really mean manipulating a stack of transport-protocol pairings.  And hopefully this'll happen in a version or two.

Hello Brian,
when you get an exception (with raise_exceptions = True) I put the not yet elaborated response from the connection in the raised Exception. It's quite easy to remove the response letting only the result in the Exception, but if I do that you cannot read the response in any way because with raise_exceptions = True the Exception is raised as soon as the error result is returned by the server and the response is not yet elaborated. 

To check try to remove the "response=response" assignment from line 295 in the ldap3.strategy.base.py module (in 0.9.7.4). Do you think this is a correct behaviour in case  size (and time) limit are excedeed? Do you thinks it's better to have no entries at all instead of only those that are within the limit? (This make sense because if you get some entries and a limit exception you have no ways to ask for the remaining entries, this is, I think a flaw in the LDAP protocol itself).

Bye,
Giovanni

That's typically how errors in multithreading appear.  Your program will seem to work but just randomly crash every now and then.  You really need to understand multithreading if you want to write multithreaded applications.  Either you design it correctly by static analysis or you are going to have these bugs.  The python tools for multithreading don't make this particularly easy either since they are not very well thought out.

Anyway, the most important rule when writing multithreaded applications is that you can't have two threads modifying the same thing at the same time.  So if you have threads that touch the same instance of any object and you haven't placed appropriate thread synchronization around those points then you have a bug, unless those objects are inherently immutable, which is generally not the case.

Hey Ron,

Yes basically most of the framework would be fine as is. Look at the RunMonthly algo for an example. It looks to see if the current month is different than the month the last time the algo was called. If so, it returns True, which essentially allows execution to continue. If false, the AlgoStack stops execution at that point. You could apply the same logic at the intraday level. Another interesting algo is Rebalance if you want to see how the actual allocation of capital is done.

I developed bt for longer term portfolio style strategies, where you are essentially rebalancing / shifting weights between different assets. The general pattern is Select -> Weigh -> Rebalance. So, Select figures out which securities should be in the portfolio, Weigh determines their weights (equal weight, min var., etc.) and Rebalance does just that.

You could do the same on a smaller time scale - basically, your algo would determine which securities should have some allocation (x% of your portfolio, or whatever logic you choose to apply). 

I haven't really looked at any of the other frameworks in a really long time so I can't give you good advice. The only other Python one I am familiar with would be Zipline, although I haven't used it myself. It powers the website Quantopian, which does support intraday out of the box (I think). 

Hope this helps,
Phil

So if the UART on the upy board can't keep up with continuous bursts of buffer-size characters without dropping a character, then you need to keep dropping buffer-size until it does work.

On the ESP8266, a buffer-size around 30 seems to work for most people. I've not worked with the ESP32 yet, so I don't have a feel for how well the UART code is, and how much latency the interrupt handlers have.

Your example is more like using two different schemas, and is not passing kwargs down the objects. You can't do smth like this without declaring two schemas:
```
schema({
    'smth': {
          'other': Import(Use(check_pk), Use(get_instance))
    }
})
```
I build one schema with functions like `schema_for_model('app.Model')`, and it's only one clean definition without lambda and function hacks. I also don't need to know all supported kwargs when defining schemas. So in future extending it with another kwarg is easier.
Check this:
```
def schema_for_model(model):
    if isinstance(model, basestring):
        lazy_model = lambda: get_model(*model.rsplit('.', 1))
    else:
        lazy_model = lambda: model
    return And(
        Or(int, LazyType(lazy_model)),
        Import(
            Use(get_object_pk),
            Use(get_object_instance(lazy_model))
        ))
```
I don't care what kwargs it support, I just need to use it like this:
```
schema = Schema({
     'user': schema_for_model('auth_app.User'),
     'example': schema_for_model('examples.ExampleModel'),
})
```

```
schema.validate(import=True)

schema.validate(json_schema=True)
```

Fixed in 7118e406a14ade98959d8629075f0fc1965b25c0, made a release candidate `hiku==0.3.9rc1` for you for further testing, here is how to specify properly non-required options for GraphQL:
```python
Option('search', Optional[String], default=None)
```

And there is an interesting case with default values:
```python
Option('foo', Integer, default=123)
```
This option is totally valid in Hiku: it is not required, and if it will be missing in query, Hiku will use default value `123` and won't accept `null` values. But in GraphQL this option will be required. As your quote from specification says:

> If the argument type is non‐null the argument is required

So GraphQL here is slightly less flexible and Hiku maybe is slightly more complicated, as it gives you ability to distinguish when value is `null` and when it is missing.

Hello,
telegram caches all inline query responses for up to 5 minutes by default. this is because most of the bots search in some website and content is unlikely to change however caching could save lots of bandwidth on multiple users requesting the same queries.

but this behavior is overwritable, from https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#answerinlinequery : 
> cache_time = The maximum amount of time in seconds that the result of the inline query may be cached on the server. Defaults to 300.

telepot's API also implants this parameter for `answerInlineQuery` method. see [here](http://telepot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#telepot.Bot.answerInlineQuery).

you may also increase the cache duration for bots with deterministic results, e.g. calculators. 2+2 is always going to be 4...

So this is actually a two-fold issue, I'll try to split my answer.

1. From a quick look at your patch I think the fail-safe won't work with thesubdb as thesubdb only provides the hash. At some point we need to trust the provider. If we start assuming provider give bad information this is where we're going to implement some weird code have inconsistent behavior. Then it's going to be difficult to implement new providers that fit properly in subliminal without patching subliminal's logic again. If trust issues have to be solved, it should be in a per-provider basis, not subliminal-wide. In the case of OSS, [here](https://github.com/Diaoul/subliminal/blob/master/subliminal/providers/opensubtitles.py#L52) seems a better place.
2. Have you read [OpenSubtitles API documentation](http://trac.opensubtitles.org/projects/opensubtitles/wiki/XMLRPC#SearchSubtitles)? If the documentation is accurate, what you did in your patch doesn't actually changes anything as it is ignored server side. I agree this is suboptimal.

Bot accounts aren't really for logging in and sending a single message, as you get a limited number of connects/day. It's also quite a lot of information to send/receive just for a single message. Webhooks are supported on the rewrite branch of the lib, but it's trivial to post them yourself.

It's generally assumed that you have some knowledge of how discord bots work, [but the process for creating a bot account is documented on the development branch.](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/rewrite/discord.html) Furthermore, the [examples](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/tree/async/examples) serve as a more than sufficient, well, example for just a basic bot.

@rlue It'd be nice for Reddit to remove the ambiguity, though it's not essential.  I'm happy to consider Reddit in error if rtv implements HTML-style comments.

@michael-lazar More generally, and correct me if I'm wrong, what's necessary here is for rtv to be able to unambiguously (or statistically-reasonably-ambiguously) create a comment block which is used for tracking post state, and remove that content before submitting the post or comment to Reddit.

You're presently tracking state within the content body itself.  One option would be to preserve state elsewhere within the app.  If that's possible, it bypasses the problem entirely.

Having the metadata _in_ the body has actually proved useful to me as I've been archiving my Reddit content from /r/dredmorbius, with updates, various corrections, etc.  I've [developed a workflow for this](https://old.reddit.com/r/dredmorbius/comments/8urwck/on_tools_methods_and_workflow/), mostly involving opening in a pager (`less`), and piping content via a shell script to an archive file with a standard naming convention.  Further filtering with `fdupes` and some plagiarism-detection / file-similarity tools to weed out the duplicate / multiple-version instances.  I'm over half-way through that process.

And, with metadata-in-file, I actually know the corresponding posts, which is handy.

The problem is that for about a quarter of the archive I'd been writing the "corrected" files _back_ to Reddit ... stripped of header divisions.  I make heavy use of those....

## Suggestions

1. Move metadata to the app and exclude it from the file entirely.  Possible, though not IMO ideal.

2. Implement a `\<!-- comment --\>` style tag, and rely on that for being stripped out afterward.  This risks removing any patterns matching `<!-- .* -->` within extant Reddit posts, though that should be a _relatively_ low risk.  At scale it's still likely unacceptable.

3. Escape whatever comment-delimiting tokens you select on reading, remove on writing, from the post.

4. Use HTML-style comments **plus a long, randomly-generated identification string** which is retained in-app state.  Strip the HTML-style comment whose body contains that string, on re-ingestion and writing to Reddit.

Option 4 is probably the most robust, and would be what I suggest.  2 & 3 would likely work.

Thoughts?

I found the problem... because the `parser-input-select` is being used, it automatically updates the select value in the cache using the "updateCell" method. So when extra code is included to trigger an "update", it is removing all the group header rows.

```html
    <th class="sorter-inputs">Total Cost</th>

</thead>
```

Looking at your SQL -- it looks like your second statement has multiple SQL statements in the batch, including subqueries. From what I understand about pyodbc, it uses the `SQLDescribeParam` function in the ODBC API to determine the data type of each parameter in a prepared statement -- but according to the [MSFT docs](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational-databases/native-client-odbc-api/sqldescribeparam?view=sql-server-2017), this doesn't work when there are multiple statements in the batch. With `fast_executemany=True`, pyodbc can't determine the data types of each parameter and defaults to VARCHAR(255). My hypothesis is that this is related to the behaviour you're seeing. 

I still don't know why it works in the case where `fast_executemany=False` .

I think this is the simplest way to start with.

a) we would need to know that py3status has stopped.

b) some modules trigger other modules to update  eg group, so we would have to remember all that.  Also the output is only generated when a module has updated so we shouldn't be doing to much work.

> Though, I am not totally sure whether the whole logic of py3status should go into the py3 subprocess.

I think that might be easier than trying to split things out.  The output function `Core.run()` treats i3status modules as py3modules now so needs to talk to them etc being itthe same process would make that far easier.

>  It forces people to remove the obsolete configuration parameter, otherwise they probably wouldn't.

I think it makes it seem broken. IMHO it also makes it hard to manage the depreciation of config params as we lose the ability to see what the user has set as parameters.  I think if someone actually sets the new param we should respect that rather than making it not work.

Maybe @ultrabug has a view

Personally, I think it is important to have some modules that do specific things eg `dpms` this could be done by using external script or similar, but it is nice to have the module so that users can easily access the functionality.  It is nice to have lots of modules as this shows what we can do.

We do have lots of things that we could implement without needing a module.  I often use the `static_string` module - for example to lock my screen/suspend.  This feels like making a module would be silly.  I think maybe we should create a `recipes` type section in the documentation for these type things so that users can copy/adapt them, rather than adding new  modules.

If we find that there are limitations we can always look at improving things.  eg my suspend module calls a script to lock screen then suspend, and I can't remember exactly why I couldn't put all the logic straight into the onclick of the module.

So I'd say to answer your question "should all of these simple modules be implemented as new separate modules?"  It depends.  Generally I'd say using external script/static_string if it can all be done in the i3status.conf is the way to go.  If we need to actually call a script rather than just calling a command etc then maybe a module is better.  I think modules for key feature are ok too,  but yes maintaining all the modules takes time and energy.

Just pushed a new update. This should work as expected now but there are several things that are quite unsatisfactory:

1) It's very difficult to tests. There are currently no tests for live trading. Going forward for a robust framework we should add mocked network requests to test the brokers / stores.o

2) Due to https://github.com/mementum/backtrader/blob/482ebecc84c09a781d37345f87f4b730aedce136/backtrader/brokers/ibbroker.py#L463 it turns out that the price is already correctly updated by `commissionReport`. What is needed to actually fix the existing bug is just to prevent updating price in `updatePortfolio`. As mentioned above, apprently it is impossible to get the correct price _without commission_ from `updatePortfolio`.

3) There was one problem with the preceding commits. Considering the actual events fired for an order getting filled is like this:

```
<position>
<commissionReport>
<updatePortfolio>
```

When `<commissionReport>` gets fired `<position>` would have already updated the `size` information and `<commissionReport>` would _double the effect_ of the position due to https://github.com/mementum/backtrader/blob/482ebecc84c09a781d37345f87f4b730aedce136/backtrader/brokers/ibbroker.py#L463.

4) However, `commissionReport` is not fired on first connect. To get existing positions, we still need to use `reqPositions()` upon connect to grab the correct position and price information. When we get `positionEnd` we unsubscribe to prevent the "double effect" described above

I won't be merging this as it is.

I think there should be another abstraction layer between "file" and "version string occurrence" and a "file" could have many "version string occurrence"s like this:

```
[bumpversion:version_occurrence:some_context]
search = some context for {current_version}
file = myfile

[bumpversion:version_occurrence:another_context]
search = another context for {current_version}
file = myfile
```

.. and while I think this is very beautiful from the software architectural point of view and gets the abtractions right, it definitely adds another layer of complexity for the end user (who, in almost all cases would only want to bump one version occurrence per file).

Thanks for showing me that `[bumpversion:file]` is not the most granular config setting there is.

After some trial-and-error I got the following `.bumpversion.cfg` stanza to work. The documentation was misleading.

```
[bumpversion:file:setup.py]
search = VERSION \= '{current_version}'
replace = {new_version}
```

> I'm not totally sold on the specific suggestion of doing it at the strategy level (it seems harder than some of the alternatives)

Yeah, it occurred to me that the only way I can think of implementing `.shared()` would be to generate all the examples ahead of time and return them encoded in the result of `shared`, since hypothesis can't control the order in which tests are run. This basically means keeping them in memory forever. Maybe that's okay for some use cases, but I guess a lot of people who have problems with the time it takes to generate objects are probably generating *big* objects.

> The easiest version is probably to give you an "always save examples which satisfy assumptions in the database" mode to run in.

I don't really know how the database works, but that sounds like it's probably a better idea.

> Firstly, tests written using Hypothesis are typically a bit longer than their corresponding quickcheck implementations. I'm not sure why but suspect some combo of Python testing culture, a tendency to more non-uniform interfaces to test, and it looking more natural in the language. So you might find it productive to combine some of your tests.

Yeah, I generally dislike the large size of typical unit tests in Python ;-)

> Secondly, in general strategies built up through the standard combinators shouldn't be too slow (this isn't really true in that there are a lot of things you can do that end up asking Hypothesis for huge data). So it's quite possible that you've just hit a performance bug.

Actually I think that most likely it's because the objects I'm building up are lots and lots of [pyrsistent](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pyrsistent) objects, which are fairly fast but not as fast as built-in data structures, of course. I think that's probably a large part of why things are pretty slow.


> If you show me the strategy you're using (I think I saw an earlier version of it) I can take a look. The most likely culprit is that if you have nested collections you may wish to turn down the size of the interior values (either with an average_size or max_size parameter).

Here's the strategy:

https://gist.github.com/radix/1124c4c60e4616b791da

1. I realized while putting that gist together that the way I'm building locations is (still) dumb, since it can accidentally create a location with the same name twice. Since later they're put into a dict based on name, that means I'm basically doing some work that is going to overwritten if some location names clash.
2. I set average and max sizes for collections -- mostly just so the examples were tractable, since trying to debug my game states was becoming pretty difficult with the tons and tons of goop I was getting printed out :)
3. if you have any other feedback about improving those strategies I would massively appreciate hearing it.

Just as a little background: I'm not personally blocked by any performance issues with strategy generation; this kind of example-sharing just seemed like a good idea, since it seems like there's not a lot of benefit in generating different samples for different tests (at least in this kind of scenario). This code I'm working on is actually for a screencast I'm creating on Functional Programming in Python for O'Reilly, where the running example is a trivial text adventure game. So this isn't a serious app, just an educational example.

@kennethreitz @kennethreitz

Records uses sqlalchemy's `text()` function around for every query. This is what interprets anything starting with a colon as a bindparam, like you said. 

From the [sqlalchemy documentation](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/sqlelement.html#sqlalchemy.sql.expression.text)
> For SQL statements where a colon is required verbatim, as within an inline string, use a backslash to escape
> `t = text("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='\:username'")`

So use backslash to escape the colon. Alternatively, I would recommend using parameterization instead.

Once created, the menu structure cannot be modified. 

However, all attributes of menu items can be set to callables providing the actual value when necessary; this is the intended way to make menus dynamic (see [here](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pystray/blob/master/tests/icon_tests.py#L346))

Since I don't know your use case, I cannot give you a definitive answer, but I suggest you look into either letting the visibility of several items be toggled to make only one visible---this has the advantage of letting you easily specify different handler for different displayed menu item texts---or let the actual text be dynamic.

If you modify the state needed to calculate the properties of the menu items outside of an activation callback, make sure to call [update_menu](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pystray/blob/master/lib/pystray/_base.py#L188) to allow the menu to be updated on all platforms.

I would say the stdlib is lacking some very useful functionality in this area. Since it's it's not specific to to an application domain, it makes sense for the stdlib to provide it natively. PEP 526 gives a certain impetus to this desire, since now it *might* be possible to design something where you can simply write
```py
x: int
```
(in some decorated class) as opposed to
```py
x = attr.ib(validator=attr.validators.instance_of(int))
```
It would also probably be a good idea to have something in the stdlib so that people aren't tempted to abuse `collections.namedtuple` (or its typed cousing `typing.NamedTuple`) when they are looking for a notational shorthand.

It is possible that *attrs* is the "gold standard" for this functionality -- but it is neither the first nor the last library of this sort. And it should never be simply promoted to stdlib status -- that would be terrible for all involved. So I think it's entirely reasonable that we try to extract the good ideas from *attrs*, combine them with some other good ideas, and try to package them into something that befits the standard library, without trying to preserve any kind of backwards compatibility with *attrs* itself. All this should be done while exercising restraint in feature abundance, favoring careful design for extensibility over including some of the more advanced features.

The exercise is still in its early stages, but I see no reason to stop because *attrs* already exists.

You can't access the config before your apps are loaded, this basically means you should not access any config attribute at import time.

```python
# module.py

# This will raise AttributeError
my_global = config.backup_time

def run_on_load():
    # This will raise AttributeError, see below
    if config.backup_time:
        pass
run_on_load()  # run on first import

class MyClass:
    # This will raise AttributeError
    my_class_attr = config.backup_time    # evaluated on first import
```

You should be able to access the config within methods and functions that are not called on import time.

I agree this is not ideal. The assumption has been that `ErrorAccessDenied` is a general error that would happen for all items returned, but apparently that's not the case.

You could try to allow this error in the response instead of raising it. Try adding this at the top of your code:

```python
from exchangelib.services import GetItem
from exchangelib.errors import ErrorAccessDenied
GetItem.ERRORS_TO_CATCH_IN_RESPONSE += (ErrorAccessDenied,)
```

This should result in the private items being returned as `ErrorAccessDenied` instances when you iterate over the results of the query, instead of `ErrorAccessDenied` being raised directly. You can then decide to raise the exception instance or ignore it, as appropriate for your use case.

Hi,

The autoregister thing for proxy models was taken out because, as it turned out, it was somewhat broken. If it was working for you, then excellent! But in a lot of cases it wasn't.

The issue is that django's a bit confused about proxy models. The actual proxy model has it's own ContentType entry in the database, but ContentType.objects.get_for_model(Model) returns the content type of the parent model. I lodged a bug in the Django issue tracker a long time ago about this:

https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/11154

While this inconsistent handling of content types and proxy models exists, django-reversion is going to give inconsistent behaviour in it's version handling. If you can think of a consistent solution to this issue, I'd love to hear it.

```yaml
defaults:
```

`~/.ssh/assh.d/bob.yml`

```yaml
file-templates:
- bob-hosts
```

This will fail for non-ASCII characters in any of the affected strings (eg connection string parameters).

Unlike the % operator which promotes any ASCII-bytes+Unicode string to Unicode, str.format always forces the format arguments into the type of the format string, causing a UnicodeEncodeError for non-ASCII. That makes sense in Python 3 code where byte strings are the exception, but it badly breaks a lot of Python 2 code where byte strings are being used as a default ASCII test type.

It is not a good idea to blindly blanket-change % to .format for this reason. The related links seem to be dead now but if this is what quantifiedcode is advocating, please don't.

If you enable [CSRF_COOKIE_HTTPONLY](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/settings/#std:setting-CSRF_COOKIE_HTTPONLY), the HTTP header that sets the CSRF cookie looks like this:

```
Set-Cookie: csrftoken=bfabf0b1c1133a822; path=/; HttpOnly
```

The `HttpOnly` suffix means that Javascript cannot access the cookie using `document.cookie`. This is to mitigate against XSS attacks: if someone manages to inject Javascript, they still won't be able to access the CSRF token in the cookie. Here's a good blog post about HttpOnly: http://blog.codinghorror.com/protecting-your-cookies-httponly/

If you wanted to make this package compatible with that setting, you would have to serve the CSRF token somewhere in the DOM, so that the Javascript can access it there. If someone were to inject Javascript maliciously, they could also get the token, but that already can happen for any Django form which includes the CSRF token as a hidden input. (Yes, that does seem to make CSRF_COOKIE_HTTPONLY pretty pointless, there was some discussion about that in the mailing list but nothing came of it.)

I'm definitely not ruling out adding these parameters in future, but frequently people ask about using them when in nearly all circumstances the chosen defaults are sufficient.

If you'd like some more information about how these parameters can influence behaviour in an impaired system you might want to look at:

* [CouchDB 2.0's read and write behaviour in a cluster](http://www.dx13.co.uk/articles/2015/10/19/couchdb-20s-read-and-write-behaviour-in-a-cluster.html) 
* The section on _Impaired nodes_ in [CouchDB 2.0 - The awkward bits](http://events.linuxfoundation.org/sites/events/files/slides/ABDE_mike_wallace_couchdb_awkward_bits.pdf#53)

These highlight some of the difficulties faced when the quorum parameters are used to try and achieve some kind of stronger consistency. Ultimately the system is *eventually* consistent and applications need to tolerate that.

Desirable new features would be:
- Allow packed sequence data or padding handling for variable length sequences.
- Support LSTM-projection for large-scale RNNs.
- RNN state clipping.

Desirable performance improvements would be:
- Allow down conversion to enable HMMA when possible.
- Allow algorithm search for performance tuning through `findIntensity`.
- Reuse the space for random states when dropout is enabled.

Finally found some time to continue with this.
1) Rebased on recent master
2) Fixed test according to https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/pull/64#issuecomment-170170418
3) Integration tests for unmarshaling

The good news is that unmarshaling doesn't seem to be affected (see matrix in https://github.com/andreashug/bravado-core/blob/c581aaba8924afe340f124375015afc9f5743762/tests/unmarshal/unmarshal_nullable_test.py)

The not so good news however is, I'm not sure how marshaling should behave in combination with `x-nullable`. Currently, `marshal_object()` drops keys whose value is `None`, which leads to a ValidationError (required property) -> https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/7d13d2512f85983eca086db1eb0ddab797b22499/bravado_core/marshal.py#L129-L130. One might think, it should keep the key if `x-nullable` is `True`. Or we could say that `x-nullable` is only for properties and not accounted in parameter specs.

Any opinions on that?

If plone.app.event 2.0 is for Plone 5 only, we can indeed move to plone domain in plone.app.locales master.
The steps to accomplish this if you want to do it are the following:

- modify the plone.app.event code to use the plone domain
- modify experimental/i18n.cfg in buildout.coredev 5.0 to include plone.app.event in the plone domain.
- bin/buildout -c experimental/i18n.cfg
- modify your local zope.tal to be able to extract messages from templates that use tal:attributes chameleon functionality (see the top of experimental/i18n.cfg). If you don't do it, you will have messages removed.
- use
  bin/i18n plone
  to regenerate plone.pot and resync plone files.
- You can then use "i18ndude admix" command to transfer translated messages from plone.app.event.po to plone.po for each language.

It seems like this was due to a bad interaction of impyla and a multiprocessing pool. Essentially, we were sharing the result of calling `impala.dbapi.connect()`  across subprocesses, and then creating a `cur = self.conn.cursor()` within each subprocess.

Here's a stripped down example:

https://gist.github.com/natekupp/33bdc7b7324e68afbe8f

We've moved the instantiation of the impala object to within a run() function called by the multiprocessing pool, which seems to have resolved this crash.

>What the heck I'm doing wrong?!

Copying the source directory is not installing pyzmq. pyzmq, like other Python packages, should be installed via the standard Python tool `pip`, e.g.:

    pip install pyzmq

Putting the `zmq` source directory in the working directory will only prevent zmq from ever working.

Supporting Python 2.5 may be difficult, but if you download the source for the old version and install with `--zmq=/usr/local`, it should work. Note that it will require old libzmq as well (2.x, I believe).

This one still takes an extra ctrl-C to kill, because the thread.join() that happens during cleanup is just hanging...I don't know why. thread.is_alive is returning True and yet printlines in the thread's mainloop show it is not active.

I wasn't able to figure out what solves the problem in all cases, but my use case is solved by just not hanging onto a reference to the authenticator:

```python
import zmq
from zmq.auth.thread import ThreadAuthenticator

class Context(zmq.Context):
    auth = None
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        zmq.Context.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)
        auth = ThreadAuthenticator(self)
        auth.start()

context = Context()
server = context.socket(zmq.PULL)
port = server.bind_to_random_port('tcp://*')

client = context.socket(zmq.PUSH)
client.connect('tcp://localhost:%d' % port)

for i in range(10):
    msg = b'hello'
    client.send(b'hello')
    assert server.recv() == msg
print('done')
```

That's a distinction between client-side validation and server-side validation. We never validate max constraints on the client side because that makes the sdk forwards-incompatible if the service ever increases the length (as EC2 did fairly recently with instance ids). We can't know for sure what the limitations on the endpoint you're connecting to are, so we report what we will validate. If you're too long the service will respond with an error, which will bubble back as a `ClientError`.

With the release of boto3 1.4.0, we introduced a managed ``copy()`` method that handles the copying of multipart and nonmultipart objects: https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/s3.html#copies. Each of these methods make use of ``CopySource`` being a dictionary consisting of a bucket, key, and optional version id. Using this dictionary, we will create the proper ``CopySource`` key for you so you do not worry how to format or url encode the string anymore. Therefore, resolving the issue.

This worked for me:

    import boto3
    client = boto3.client('s3')
    bucket = "bucket_name"
    key = "file_key"
    k = client.head_object(Bucket = bucket, Key = key)
    m = k["Metadata"]
    m["new_metadata"] = "value"
    client.copy_object(Bucket = bucket, Key = key, CopySource = bucket + '/' + key, Metadata = m, MetadataDirective='REPLACE')

Consider this change:
```python

import boto3
import boto3.session

B = 'symbols-public-dev'
K_base = 'test_boto3_issue_1128_{}.txt'
import random
for i in range(10):
    K = K_base.format(random.random())
    try:
        s3.head_object(
            Bucket=B,
            Key=K,
        )
        print("EXISTED ALREADY")
    except ClientError as exc:
        if exc.response['Error']['Code'] == '404':
            print("DID NOT EXIST")
        else:
            raise
    s3.put_object(Bucket=B, Key=K, Body=b'asdfasdf')
```

Now, the filename (aka. key name) is always different so every time it checks if the file is there, it concludes that it needs to do the `s3.put_object`.

I recall from reading another isssue that the problem is that the process object is not being deleted
quickly under python3. This is because of the change in garbage collection between py2 and py3.

Looking at the code it looks like the fix is to explicitly del the process object once it is no longer needed.

This bug I suspect may happen on py2 depending on garbage collector config.

for example from cmd.py:965:

    def __get_object_header(self, cmd, ref):
        cmd.stdin.write(self._prepare_ref(ref))
        cmd.stdin.flush()
        return self._parse_object_header(cmd.stdout.readline())

should be something along these lines:

    def __get_object_header(self, cmd, ref):
        cmd.stdin.write(self._prepare_ref(ref))
        cmd.stdin.flush()
        result = self._parse_object_header(cmd.stdout.readline())
        del cmd
        return cmd

- when troubleshooting the above problem I also added some debugging stuff to the test server: now it's possible to echo all data going through the socket. Default is disabled, activate by uncommenting one line in the `start_http_server()` in "test/run_tests.py".

The ugly part i.e. the killing of the WebDriver service process could be improved to be more specific/robust by e.g.
- by checking which browser we are using and do stuff based on it
- improving the PID detection, currently it simply does this `self._current_browser().__dict__.get("know_service_attribute_name").process.pid`. Where the "know_service_attribute_name" is "service" for GC and "iedriver" for IE. This could also work for other browsers that use the same attribute name for the service.
- manage different FF versions e.g. v46 without driver and newer ones with geckodriver

djangocms installer provides:
* a command line argument to force a specific timezone (http://djangocms-installer.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#wizard-arguments)
* fallbacks to ``UTC`` if cannot discover local timezone

### config-file

Sample ini config with all available arguments https://github.com/frost-nzcr4/djangocms-installer/blob/ini-parse/config.ini.sample.
Run:

```ShellSession
djangocms_installer --config-file /path/to/config.ini project_name
```

General config can be cutomised with any available arguments:

```ShellSession
djangocms_installer --config-file /path/general-config.ini -p /path/other/proj -s -q project_name
```

If config.ini not contains `no-input = true` and `-q` argument isn't set then one act as a placeholder with default values for wizard.

### config-dump

Dumps all arguments to custom config.ini:

```ShellSession
djangocms_installer --config-dump /path/config.ini -p . project_name
```

if installation fails dump can be used to fix some arguments and re-run installer with dumped config:

```ShellSession
djangocms_installer --config-dump /path/config.ini --db postgres://wrong-usr:pwd@host/db -p . project_name
# failed
djangocms_installer --config-file /path/config.ini --db postgres://correct-user:pwd@host/db -p . project_name
# succeed
```

I feel the “have a requirements.txt but not install everything” feature is an edge case. You can create the Pipfile manually in this case (`touch Pipfile` if you are on POSIX) to avoid the detection, or if #2188 is accepted, use the `init` command with the same effect.

Regarding to the auto detection, however, I do find the current “search in parent directories” behaviour counter-intuitive. The auto-import only happens when you first initialise the environment (i.e. don’t have a Pipfile yet), and in almost all situations you would do it in the project root, because that just makes sense.

How Pipenv converts requirements.txt in a parent directory is also not very consistent with itself. Say I have this file structure:

```
parent
├── project
└── requirements.txt
```

When I run `pipenv install` in `project`, `parent/requirements.txt` will be imported, *but the resulting Pipfile would be in `project`*. If we search requirements.txt in parent directories, we ought to consider where we find it the project root, and create the Pipfile in that directory instead. If initialisation always create Pipfile in the current directory, then we need to treat the directory where we initialise as the project root, and don’t search outwards for requirements.txt.

So there are two ways to fix this inconsistency (that I can think of):

* Initialisation should create Pipfile in the same directory where if finds a requirements.txt, if there is one.
* Initialisation should only consider requirements.txt in the current directory, not in parent directories. The result Pipfile will be created where you run the initialisation command (the current behaviour).

All right, I have a demonstrating `Dockerfile` now ready.
It prepares the following, common setting for a user that might user conda or virtual env:

### System-Wide Software

A default CPython 2.7 build with some version of gcc (e.g. 5) and building some version of cython.

### Virtual / Per-User Software

A conda provided CPython 2.7 build with some other version of gcc (4.8) and *without* cython.

### The Issue

The problem now is, that setuptools will pick on `build_ext` the system-wide install of cython.This breaks e.g. the build of matplotlib.

This effect is commonly known as side-effect during install of e.g. C/C++ libraries and instead of a "pick any" of cython an option to disable/pick-a-specific cython would be needed for better control.

### Demonstrator

**bash**
```bash
docker build -t setuptools .
```

**docker**
```Dockerfile
# general environment for docker
ENV        DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive \
           FORCE_UNSAFE_CONFIGURE=1

# install minimal spack depedencies
RUN        apt-get update \
           && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
              bzip2 \
              build-essential \
              pkg-config \
              libc6-dev \
              python-minimal \
              python-dev \
              python-pip \
              ca-certificates \
              poppler-utils \
              coreutils \
              curl \
              git \
              lsb-release \
              wget \
           && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN        pip install setuptools --user --no-binary :all:
RUN        pip install cython --user --no-binary :all:
RUN        python --version && which python
RUN        /root/.local/bin/cython --version

RUN        wget http://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -O miniconda.sh && \
           chmod +x miniconda.sh && \
           ./miniconda.sh -b && \
           ls

RUN        git clone --depth 10 https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib.git

RUN        /root/miniconda2/bin/conda create -y -p /tmp/py27 python=2.7 && \
           /bin/bash -l -c "source /root/miniconda2/bin/activate /tmp/py27 && \
           /root/miniconda2/bin/conda install -y gcc libgcc=4.8.5 numpy pkgconfig libpng freetype && \
           cd matplotlib && \
           which python && \
           python setup.py build && \
           python setup.py install"
```

```python
json_str = re.sub(

                repl=r'":""\2',
                string=json_str
            )
```

> I tend to agree with @frecar that it's only through the PR comments you get the real nuances of how well reviewed the code was and by how many.

Sure, one has always go to the PR to see the details about some past issue.   That's natural, but that's also the bottom of the stack when hunting a bug.  Consider two things:

- *Unstructured information in PR reviews.*  When someone writes "LGTM" or "shipit", you cannot extrapolate whether this was a brief look, a feature test, or a detailed code review.  Having convention about using separate ``Tested-by`` and ``Reviewed-by`` signatures introduces common vocabulary that permits to distinguish these cases.  

(The question is will we care?  Looking at the current usage, 259 test signatures vs 651 review signatures, seems to indicate that the difference is important.)

- *Lack of easy local overview in the future bug hunting.*  When one reads the commit log range SHA1a..SHA1b, if the commits also contain QA information, one immediately has a good overview of what commits do, which features or bugs they fixed, and also how this was tested.  This is more efficient when compared to having to look up everything in the issue tracker individually.  

(We had similar arguments in RFC #2856 about where to store the description of new features and compatibility notes etc, whether in the commit logs or in the issue tracker.)

Hi. At the first glance your proposition seems very interesting and I thought that we might go along with something like this. However - after taking a deeper look into the djoser codebase I'm afraid that there is a bigger problem in djoser codebase.

We are making an assumption that `is_active` field (or property) exists, which is plain wrong, because `AbstractBaseUser` model class which we support does not require its implementation to provide this field. Therefore, instead of going your way I think that we need to figure out some other, more flexible solution to this problem, because at the moment djoser is simply broken for some users.

@mmalohlava 
1. Indeed, if there are many columns and all of them are accessed at once, then we will have many memory page misses. However this would be true for the old NFF format too: if every column is in a separate file, and you access all those columns at once, then each of those files will also be a page miss. If anything, the Jay format has a benefit here: if the number of rows is small then several columns have a chance of residing in the same memory page, whereas NFF does not have this advantage.

2. Vertical splitting is an approach used in Arrow and Parquet and other formats. However, I don't see what the advantages would be, and how they can outweigh the costs of such approach. The cost will be that we no longer have a `Column` as a contiguous region of memory, but instead it will be a chunked structure. This means that in order to access any element of the array, it has to be located within the chunk index first.
In theory, we could have data chunked on disk, and then reconstructed back into a contiguous range through careful memory-mapping, but this again brings back the problem of having too many memory-mapped regions at once, which we know causes problems...

3. Any. We are not going to support FAT32, so there is really no upper limit on a file size. In fact Jay file will take less space on disk compared to NFF, because the latter has many files each possibly wasting some disk space due to real file sizes being usually greater than the visible file size.

The rel="amphtml" shows as X because the owner of the site (I created this AMP page for a client) has not included the rel="amphtml" tag in the canonical document. That is probably why the Implementation is X too. 

On [another AMP page ](https://www.silverthorneattorneys.com/index.amp.html) I did for a client, when I test through this tool, all is green. Here both the original document and the amp page have the proper rel tags. 

The CDN that flashes green means that Google has already crawled the page and added it to their own CDN for AMP pages automatically. This takes 3-4 days to happen, after the AMP page is live and validated without problems. 

I didn't bother with optimizing the AMP page for Pagespeed Insights other than the usual CSS/JS minification and image optimization, as AMP pages are already ultra-fast.

Generally, if you want to pass the 'above the fold content' rule, You should inline all CSS that is needed for the above the fold content in the head of your document and async load all other CSS files (stylesheets and fonts, like FontAwesome) in the bottom of the file. You can see an example where I achieved that here (https://whidev.com/yl/). If you run this in Pagespeed Insights it gets double 100/100 score and of course, loads instantly.

It seems pip-accel will install whatever is in its cache, regardless of whether there's a new version in its index, unless you specify the version you want to install. Interestingly, it also does this if you try to do something like `pip-accel install --upgrade ./package-1.2.3.tar.gz`, which had me quite confused.

This kinda makes sense, if I understand how it works. I'd assume it treats every requirement the same, and if the package I'm installing specifies a requirement without a version specifier, I *want* it to just use whatever's in the cache. It'd be nice if this behavior could be overridden to have it ignore the cache just for the package in the actual command when the `--upgrade` flag is given, regardless of whether a version specifier is given. I don't know how feasible that is.

Failing that, the way I've scripted around this is to run `rm $(python -c 'from pip_accel.config import Config; print Config().data_directory')/sources/$PACKAGE_NAME*` before the install command to manually clear it from the cache.

This command only gets triggered on replicas, never on the master.
I read about it in [this article](http://blog.endpoint.com/2014/09/pgxlog-disk-space-problem-on-postgres.html) that deals with pg_xlog filling up. But I guess I didn't see that it is meant only for cleaning up an external archive not the live pg_xlog.

From observing a 2 node cluster running in docker locally this is what is happening:
- master cleans its own pg_xlog from time to time
- replica doesn't and all files that were cleaned up on master get made into .ready files on the replica.

With the archive_cleanup_command pointing locally those files get cleaned up too.
Failover and recovery continue to work with that command configured. If this is not how this option is intended to be used, I am even more confused as to how this is typically dealt with.

Not sure if you've seen the html report yet, but it does contain all that information. I just want to stress that we don't lose any of the data, it is rather just spread out at the moment. We are planning to have it all unified in the generated reports (and by extension the python API output): 

- The results of the tests,
- the data from the tests (c/p'able in several formats (Matlab, Python),
- how the results were calculated,
- and what purpose the test served.

The binary output that you see in the console, is mostly the raw `pytest` output. This is the package we use to automize the whole testing suite with continuous integration. I'm not sure yet if the output there will ever be as rich as the html reports.

    ```python
    ent = Entity(key=key1)
    ```

@zakons I reviewed, PTAL.

Let's lay out the risks of both sides, and see how likely each will happen:
* Solution A. Return DENIED on server failure, the risk is a local user won't be able to login.
* Solution B. Return SUCCESS, the risk is an OSLogin user *with their pubkey stored at /home/\<user\>/.ssh/authorized_keys* will be able to log in.

The risk of Solution A can be realized when there is a *persistent* server failure, and the user info is not already cached by metadata server. Which is highly probable imo.

The risk of Solution B can be realized when there is a *transient* server failure, where the NSS call passes, but the following PAM call fails. Given that both calls are retried, and the already restricting prerequisite of it, I think it will be very unlikely to happen in practice. And if this is only a regular user, the damage of the potential bad login won't be too dramatic either.

So I am leaning towards Solution B (current implementation). WDYT?

I think we need a concrete union type for the results of tasks like parsing where the actual types are determined at runtime. For me the question is how the union types and their members interact with other tasks.

one proposal would be to model it as though there's a cast/typecheck function that turns a union type'd concrete object into either a member type or doesn't match.
We could then use that to allow tasks that produce a union to fulfill selects for members of the union. The tricky part there is that it wouldn't apply for subset selects, ie if a select selected for a subset of a union, it wouldn't match. I think this is a reasonable behavior though.

A union is a type you can consume as a type, or you can consume a member, but it may or may not be fulfilled via the task that produces the union.
If you have a separate union that is a subset of another union, you would need to create a task to convert. We could add casts for subsets too, but I'd prefer to start without and see where that takes us.

How's this for a proposal:

Get rid of type constraints. Instead we'll only have something called a ProductType.
* ProductTypes are either singular, concrete types, or unions.
* A Select's slot for a ProductType can only consume one type.
* A Task can produce only one ProductType.
* If a Task produces a ProductType that is a union, we inject cast/type-checks for each of the member types of the union into the rule graph.

Sorry this is going to be a long response. I fairly passionate about things like this :)

Why is DEV and PROD any different from DEV and BETA? If they are the same, why are the separated? How come DEV and BETA are special but not PROD? 

For a minute, just take a step back from the names of the stages. You have a pipeline with 3 stages (A, B, and C). 
Scenario one: 
A (Stack 1) -> B (Stack 2) -> C (Stack 3)
Each stage in this example is one CloudFormation stack. Between any stage, you can run integ test, performance, etc. Everything is consistent and isolated. You do not have to worry about clashing, overriding properties, affect any traffic in the next stage, if you follow least privilege the stage you are in is always that since you only change policies when a deployment happens to that stage (goes back to clashing and overriding), etc. When you want to add a new stage, it is almost trivial. Meaning, add Stage D with Stack 4 anywhere in the process and nothing has to change (maybe deployment configuration and what you will pass into the stack but it is minimal).

Scenario two:
A (Stack 1) -> B (Stack 1) -> C (Stack 2)
Between any stage, you can still run integ test, performance, etc but everything is now not consistent. You are forcing to stages into one stack, so how do you handle putting a new stage in? Does it share with Stack 2 or 1? You are adding extra complexity into your deployment process. When different stages share a stack you now have to handle this in your deployment system or template. Both are unnecessarily complex. In adding new resources to your template, you need to consider how they will be in Stack 1 and Stack 2 rather than the resource is just there and it doesn't matter because each stage behaves the same.

I am sure there are things I have not considered in the first Scenario that could lean someone to use Scenario two but my (to be honest strong) opinion is scenario one. I have used this model many times now and enjoy the benefits of it. I am very heavy into scoping resources, changes, etc and scenario one gives me that platform to do it safely.


For a moment, lets go back to your original setup. That is you have one Lambda Function with two aliases (one for dev and one for prod). One thing you may not have considered in this setup is how concurrency works. Since you are using the same Lambda Function, calling the dev alias will consume concurrency from the prod alias. So running integ tests, performance, or general testing can now lead to an availability issue with your customers or on the flip side, your tests get throttled instead of your customers and now an engineer needs to spend time and find out what happened. This is a poor experience for your customer as well as wasted dev effort that could have been avoided.


I am not suggesting to not use Aliases or Versions to do things by any means. You are still free to add these to the generated Lambda Function by SAM as you wish. You may want to do [Weighted Aliases](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-traffic-shifting-using-aliases.html) in some other process outside of SAM. Or use an Alias to point to the most sable version of a function and then have one for an unstable, where each Alias points to a different Version of the API. Or maybe your Lambda Function is used as a backend for an API and you want to use Versions to version the Lambda Function with an API Version. Others in the community maybe able to give better examples of what they have used Aliases and Versions for as well. 

The point I was driving is the way you are using `AutoPublishAlias` to have multiple Aliases on a Function is not supported. This is meant to give you one Alias that always points at the more up to date Version being deployed. It is not $LATEST because $LATEST is mutable while Versions are not.

Hi @electron378, thanks for this, I've had a look over it.

I'd say it's a great start. The main thing is it doesn't look like you've referred to the Microsoft API for Word (MS API). That turns out to be a critical piece of the puzzle for avoiding mis-steps in defining the python-docx API.

If it's not immediately apparent how to do what I'm defining using the MS API, I search on something like "vba word section columns" and that will generally get me close enough to find what I'm looking for in the Word API reference: http://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/office/ff837519.aspx

In this case, it turns out to be critical, because it points to a shared object, a `PageSetup` object I believe, that appears both in sections and perhaps for the document overall. So the protocol would look something like:

```python
section = document.sections[-1]
page_setup = section.page_setup
text_columns = page_setup.text_columns
text_column = text_columns[0]
text_column.width = Inches(2)
text_column.space_after = Inches(0.25)
```

This is the neighborhood in which to look in the Word API:

https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/office/ff194295.aspx
https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/office/ff191765.aspx
https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/office/ff840256.aspx
etc. ...

It looks like there is also a `Document.PageSettings` property in the MS API, so if we add that later it can reuse the PageSettings objects we create here.

In general, I've found it best to mirror the MS API whenever possible, using pythonicized names. There are two parts to the rationale. First is that the designers put a lot of thought into it, more than we're likely to, and what they have works as a complete whole. This is a big plus, because we're adding on individual features incrementally, and its easy to "re"-design yourself into a box where you later have to change the API. Changing the API is the one big no-no in a library. Folks build things that depend on the API and if you change it you break their code.

The second reason is because at least some folks are already familiar with the MS API, so might as well not make it any harder for them than necessary.

The first step of a PMML pipeline must be able to specify the initial "feature configuration" - how many features there are, what are their names, data and operational types. Selector classes do not have this ability. So, the conversion will always fail if the first step of a PMML pipeline is some selector (any Selector class, not just `SelectKBest`).

To solve this issue, please insert `sklearn_pandas.DataFrameMapper` into your PMML pipeline as the first step:
```Python
from sklearn_pandas import DataFrameMapper
from sklearn2pmml.decoration import ContinuousDomain

data_x = df[df.columns.difference([METHOD_NAME, TARGET_NAME])]
data_y = df[TARGET_NAME]

pipeline = PMMLPipeline([
  ("mapper", DataFrameMapper([
    # Assumes that all feature columns are continuous floats
    (data_x.columns.values, ContinuousDomain())
  ]))
  ("selector", SelectorProxy(SelectKBest(chi2, k = 150))),
  ("classifier", model)
])
```

@jonboiser I think the difference is that, when the "khan academy (en)" has shared resources with the channel "portion control", and we download all the content from "portion control" and only the database from "khan academy (en)", for "khan academy (en) channel, it will has "resources_on_device" greater than 0. But since its content has never been actively downloaded by a user, its field "available" will be marked as `false`. 

I think the problem caused the issue shown in the picture is that since we don't actively import the content from channel "khan academy (en)", only the resource nodes have "available" marked as `true` but not the top level topic nodes. So I guess during export, since we check the top level's topic nodes' availability, and they are not available, the topic nodes are thus not shown here. If we can prevent the users from exporting the channels that are not actively imported by them, I guess we can avoid this issue? Please let me know if I don't explain clearly or miss anything. Thanks!

yes, @nobicycle I'm very curious whether this recurs after restarting Kolibri?

Assuming that it does not, I think our short-term fix is simply to make sure we're catching this and any other unhandled errors correctly and flagging the outstanding iceqube transfer as 'failed' when they happen happens.

Note that this is not as simple as invalidating the recommendations cache once content has been interacted with, as we have deliberately maintained the heavy caching on the recommendation endpoints as they are intensive.

The desired solution here is for us to have more aggressive server side caching of recommendations.

My suggestion is that Kolibri generated personalized recommendations are put into a separate Model that these endpoints now return, and which are updated by, for example, engaging with content.

Added a fix for this in b0108efa82695481293962b58ba6584e2a02d8a1 and tested without JS.

When `/memento/*/?url=foo.com` was requested, the normal handler `showMementosForURIRs(urir)` with route `/memento/*/<path:urir>` is not called due to the `path:` requirement specification. Instead, `show_uri(path, datetime=None)` would be called with the more generic route.

This is indeed the Griffin-Lim criterion. It is almost as you say: you are trying to find the time-domain signal whose STFT will be closest to your (modified) STFT measurements. This is most useful when the modifications are non-linear (like artefacts from the circular convolution). If the transformation is linear (like filtering is), then it is still possible to get perfect reconstruction without the need of a synthesis window.
(Note that the inverted signal will never be complex if you impose conjugate symmetry of the spectrum)

I'm not sure how else we could do this, though -- the `@label` attribute can be free text, so you will always have this problem if the encoder has taken the label from a printed score, for example, and it says something like "Posaune" or "Faggotti". So automatic mapping to MIDI instruments from the values of `@label` is a pretty unstable method.

The alternative is that we simply render everything in "Piano" (000) _unless_ it has an `instrDef@midi.instrname`definition. That's perhaps the most stable method, but you will still need to edit your MEI files.
I still don't think I'll be able to get to this anytime soon -- I think it's best to run midi files through a better MIDI -> MusicXML converter (musescore is scriptable) and load those first.

Thanks; it's good to bring up performance early and often. There will be zero change for the numba code. The Python linkers should get slightly slower because of all the squaring they have to do. If we squared the distances once and saved the result, this correction would incur a smaller cost. But there is a larger question of our policy on performance. My own take is that there are very few users who both care acutely about performance (and so will feel the pain of a few percent slowdown), and cannot use numba. But I could easily be wrong. To me, the Python linkers serve as fallbacks with reasonable performance, but also as reference implementations that are much easier to read and debug than the ugly numba version.

If we all think that way and we wanted to have a project-wide guideline for performance, it might be:

"If at all possible, PRs should not slow down 

* 2D or 3D feature-finding (i.e. both Python and numba)
* Linking with cKDTree + numba, for 'production' settings (e.g. no diagnostics). 

Everywhere else, it's acceptable to lose performance in proportion with other gains."

With "slow down" as determined by the applicable asv benchmarks (we need 3D!). But you'll notice that I am making up policy on the spot to avoid doing work. So we should either discuss this idea or ignore it altogether!

"The database" in this case implies connecting to the database set up in the relevant `/deis/database` etcd keyspace, similar to how the controller connects. The database can easily be swapped out for an RDS instance by modifying the keys set at the [database settings page](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/customizing_deis/database_settings/) and shutting off `deis-database`.

This is a loose requirement. Just like the controller, we can just connect to the database via the relevant etcd keyspace so we don't have a hard requirement on Deis' provided database, but it would be used by default for those who don't want to offload their services.

The cause of this is etcd not answering requests. We have to refresh the etcd host/port mappings frequently or else they expire out of etcd. When they expire, SSH traffic can no longer be routed from the edge of the cluster to the builder, and then all pushes to builder fail.

I know @mboersma is working on some fixes to etcd right now. I'll consult him about this, but I am wondering if raising the TTL might help, if this is really connection related.


What happens if the contract paying for the gas receives a payment before PAYGAS is called? Then the PAYGAS call would succeed at first. But later, after the transaction reverts for some other reason, the balance is insufficient. @NIC619 what's supposed to happen in this case? Are all state changes prior to PAYGAS irreversible? Are outgoing calls prior to PAYGAS illegal? Should we postpone all of this until sharding phase 2?

The problem is that a huge number loop may be a bad idea for gas estimating. And it's inefficient to loop the validator pool.

An alternative solution: minus the new validators that join the pool recently. 
**Warning: I haven't tested those codes yet....so it probably goes wrong...!**

1. Add a new mapping `registered_period: uint256 period  -> uint256 collators_num`
In `deposit()`:

```python
@public
@payable
def deposit() -> num:

    ...

    self.validators[index] = {
        deposit: msg.value,
        addr: validator_addr,
    }
    self.num_validators += 1
    self.is_validator_deposited[validator_addr] = True

+   current_period = floor(decimal(block.number / self.period_length))
+   self.registered_period[current_period] += 1

    log.Deposit(index, validator_addr, msg.value)

    return index
```

2. Update `get_validators_max_index`. The input parameter `period` is the expected period and we assume that it won’t be called when `current_period > period`. (At least it's meaningless for collating when `current_period > period`.)

```python
@internal
def get_validators_max_index(period) -> num:
    zero_addr = 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000
    all_validator_slots_num = self.num_validators + self.empty_slots_stack_top

+   current_period = floor(decimal(block.number / self.period_length)) 
+   preparing_collators_num = 0

+   # from i = 0 to i = self.lookahead_periods
+   for i in range(self.lookahead_periods + 1):
+       preparing_collators_num += self.registered_period[period - i]
+   return all_validator_slots_num - preparing_collators_num
```

It takes additional mapping to log the period that the collator joined the pool in exchange for only 5 lookups times.

@pipermerriam @carver From what I could find. . . A `Segmentation Fault` was being thrown somewhere when running via `pypy3` - which i guess is usually caused when there's a problem with available memory. I tracked down the cause to the type hints in the nested `wrapper` function. 
Changing from ... 
`def wrapper(*args: Iterable[Any], **kwargs: Dict[str, Any]) -> Callable[..., Any]` 
to 
`def wrapper(*args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> Callable[..., Any]` did the trick. 
And i'm not sure it's super important to hint `*args` or `**kwargs` as anything more specific than `Any` (here and in the other similar prs) to make it worth fixing the `Segmentation Fault` (which seems like it could get tricky)
All the other type-hint pr's have been updated to have simpler args for nested functions and to any other suggestions posted.

```
conda create -n nanshe -c nanshe nanshe
```
It's pulling the `nomkl` feature, not the `openblas` feature. In that case, I'm inclined to believe it's *doing the right thing*. One of conda's objectives is to minimize the number of active features required to satisfy the constraints. That's rather important for handling the features used with the Windows `python` package. 

Apparently, `nanshe 0.1.0a50` isn't doing enough to enforce the `openblas` track feature.

I think that if `nanshe` itself was the one that held the `openblas` track_feature instead of `openblas` itself, it might work.

Yeah, see https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/cli/main_config.py#L318

                # Note, since conda config --add prepends, these are printed in
                # the reverse order so that entering them in this order will
                # recreate the same file

That's the historical comment.  In 4.1, I added the lowest/highest priority indicators to the printout since it was potentially confusing.

In 4.2, we've made the deliberate decision to change the behavior of `--add` to append rather than prepend.  It's a safer default for the more naive user.  There are also both `--append` and `--prepend` options to be more explicit.

I believe the ones that were in the environment previously are linked against Accelerate (system BLAS) on Mac, OpenBLAS on Linux, and MKL on Windows. In short, on Windows you should get the same result in either case.

Going forward Mac and Linux will have the same linkages, but now track the `nomkl` package. An `mkl` package has been added to those platforms so that you can use MKL linked versions. On Windows, there is no option to not use MKL so its version will appear as tracking `mkl`.

The `mkl-rt` package I don't believe is supported any more. It is getting pulled in by accident. Not sure why this happens, but have seen this also. This package is the reason you are getting old versions `numpy`, `scipy`, and `scikit-learn`. Removing it should be sufficient for getting back to the newer versions of those packages and keeping MKL if you wish.
