Because of the line in the date math section, referred to above.

I did get this clarified from the ES team, and so long as no users _try_ to use [date math](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/5.5/date-math-index-names.html) in the new name, then you can name each index whatever you want.  If, however, they want to use date math, then they must end with a dash and a number.

To verify that this works as expected, I would like to see 2 tests added that use a plain-text date math example (you don't need to do the escaping, it's handled automatically): one that passes, and one that fails because of the absence of a dash and a number at the end of the date-math-index-name.

Bonus points for a screwy new index name that combines strings for `parse_date_pattern` _and_ date math (just not nested, as I'm reasonably certain that will fail).

@erwincoumans So I've got a task scheduler implemented on the ThreadSupportInterface working pretty well now (only tested on Windows though). However, I've noticed a few issues with the ThreadSupport implementations:

- The win32 version was setting the thread affinity mask of all threads to 2. This resulted in terrible performance as all the threads were trying to run on the same processor. I've commented that out for now, and the performance improved greatly.
- Why is the setNumTasks() function there? It does nothing in the Posix version and almost nothing on the win32 side
- The win32 version uses WaitForMultipleObjects in a number of places which is limited to 64 objects. So it will break if it tries to use more than 64 threads -- and there are CPUs now with more hardware threads than that
- I find the naming of getNumTasks() confusing. Seems like it should be called getNumThreads()

It just seems like the interface could be cleaned up a bit. However I don't want to expand the scope of this PR too much, so I'm leaving the interface alone.

For now I've added the task scheduler source (btTaskScheduler.h/cpp) in the same directory as the thread support files (examples/Multithreading)

My suggestion here is, lets keep the timeout feature but lets improve the API allowing the developer to make a diagnose of the issue.

So the main thing to address here is the extract step, in that it needs to be able to handle more generic cases (If not out-of-the-box at least via plugins). I typing out few cases [here](https://gist.github.com/lgpage/17e85800687b0aeac3b5b824c4b92b02) (that come from the comments) just so I can get things straight in my head and I realized this could get unnecessarily messy.

So I propose a different approach:
Modify the default extract process to "walk" all ``.zip`` files and keep the ``.zip`` file structure, for example:
- ``downloaded/assignment_id/archive/main_download.zip``
  - ``first_last_{student-id}_timestamp.zip`` (for each student - for each attempt)
    - {file-id}.ext (per release file)
    - timestamp.txt
should get extracted to:
- ``downloaded/assignment_id/extracted/main_download/``
  - ``first_last_{student-id}_timestamp/`` (for each student - for each attempt)
    - {file-id}.ext (per release file)
    - timestamp.txt

Then the collect process remains as is and the ``student_id`` only needs to provided in the collect step.

>Currently it seems that ``zip_collect`` makes the assumption that for every archive:
>- It is a flat archive and contains no subdirectories
>- All the files in the archive are named in a way that the student id can be recovered from the filename. You can write your own configurable plugin, but it still is only given the filename which means it can't do much else (e.g. get the student id from the original archive name instead).

The collect process does walk ``downloaded/assignment_id/extracted/`` and passes the absolute path to the default plugin. Granted the default plugin only applies the regexp to the base filename, but this can easily be changed.

> I think unfortunately we're going to have to add support for another type of plugin, which is an extractor plugin.

I do agree the extract code should be in a plugin just in case the default is not good enough.

For packaging the contrib repo, I think it can still persist through this change to enable-by-default, as long as we update its install script to retain the existing not-enabling behaviour. In order to make the big-bundle paradigm redundant, though, I think we'd need a centralized catalogue to make nbextension packages discoverable over the net. The obvious choice for me would be to search _both_ npm and pypi for specific tags, but then again, perhaps some filtering would be a good idea? Maybe an 'official' github repo that just lists extension URIs? Anyway, this cataloguing is, I think, a separate enough issue that the enable-by-default can proceed without addressing it yet.

> Perhaps, looking at the metadata discussion above, the right on-disk metadata and documentation format is... the notebook! I think if an extension packed a well-named notebook, with some metadata (visually edited and previewed by the configurator), we'd be able leverage all of the existing machinery and let people make some really nice stuff.

Hmm. Interesting idea, you mean the configurator would take over some of the notebook rendering in order to make the metadata attributes editable? This would certainly allow you to mix together arbitrary code for altering/setting metadata with markdown readme-type text, and even examples of the new extension in action. Feels like a fairly extensive bit of work to get it going though, so I'll leave it for now :grimacing: but if it seems like it'd be valuable, I'd be happy to have a stab at it in future.

> Better, keep the versions around so you can go back to them in case of fail (so don't make the configurator an nbextension, i guess?).

This is a neat idea. Currently, jupyter_nbextensions_configurator uses the notebook's javascript config API, which provides no history. The package does already provide serverextensions to add additional handlers, so it would be possible to add a config-with-history API, I guess. In fact, alterations it makes could be stored in a dedicated config file, accessed through the regular config API, I suppose :thinking: I think history is probably beyond the scope of the current issue, but potentially something helpful & interesting to consider for another day.

So, I think I'll proceed as follows, unless anyone thinks of reasons to change:

* adopt versioning for compatibility, as suggested by @maartenbreddels 
* use the configurator's discovery mechanism to find installed extensions, then apply explicit enable/disable statuses, as set in config in the current notebook's manner. This would mean that extensions are 'enabled' (in the sense of loading) by default, but that a custom installer script (e.g. for contrib repo) can still override this by explicitly disabling at install time anything which has not already been explicitly _en_abled

As for the yaml/json decision, how about we:

 * use config stored in a `package.json` file, under a specific key, say (following [this SO suggestion on npm key naming](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/10065564/add-custom-metadata-or-config-to-package-json-is-it-valid)) `org_jupyter.nbextension`. This is simple to implement, and since the configurator's data isn't expected to ever be edited anyway (it sets out only the defaults & types of values stored through the notebook config API), no end-user should really have to do any editing of it anyway, unless they're developing an extension, in which case I think we can fairly assume that they are able to familiarise themselves with the json syntax.
 * additionally support the existing yaml config format *if yaml is importable*. This way, bundles like contrib can add yaml as a dependency without forcing it on notebook as a whole.

Thanks for your previous comment. I've poked around for some more memory savings for my application and I've done a couple of things:

Firstly, after the line
`cur_outgrad = node.sum_outgrads()
`
I've added
`node.outgrads = None
`
which stops the derivatives accumulating in memory after being used (though I don't see why they weren't being deleted along with the node), and also
`node.node_value = 0.
`
which frees up memory as it runs the tape. While this isn't really necessary I found it helpful in memory critical applications.
 
Secondly, and more importantly, following your advice of creating fewer temporaries I changed things like
`np.sum(A[:, na, :] * B[:, na, :], 0)
`to
`np.einsum('ab,ac->bc', A, B)
`
This made a huge (i.e. factor _a_) difference

I think this error is happening when peru wants to print fancy unicode bracket characters, like this:

```
┌ a
├ b
└ c
```

The immediate workaround is to run peru with `-q`/`--quiet` or `-v`/`--verbose`, so that it stops trying to be fancy. Quiet is also the default if peru is running with its stdout redirected to a file.

It sounds like your terminal is configured in ASCII mode, without Unicode support. If you run

```bash
python3 -c "import sys; print(sys.stdout.encoding)"
```

does it say ASCII?

If you run

```bash
python3 -c "print('日本語')"
```

does it fail with the same error?

@thewtex it's very exciting --- the whole `setup.py` has less than 200 lines and it essentially uses messy distutils or setuptools to figure out what needs to be done, a then calls cmake to get it done, including installation on all platforms (Linux, Mac, Windows).

@scopatz We have now separated the Python wrappers into https://github.com/symengine/symengine.py, they require the pure C++ library https://github.com/symengine/symengine to be already installed. I first thought I can just use distutils without cmake, since all we need now is just a simple cython based wrapper. But then you still need to link it properly on all platforms, and things quickly become messy again. So we decided to use cmake anyway, even though our package is now essentially a regular Python package, with no C or C++ files except the Cython generated wrapper. Another advantage is that I actually use cmake directly, without setup.py, and everything works, including installation. But somebody else might prefer setup.py, which is now just a thin layer on top of cmake.

Coverage went down because of changes to `ModularVisualization.py`. There is a change here that is of consequence when it comes to loading JavaScript packages that are included locally. Instead of using a `set`, using a `list` encourages ordering of `package_includes` from visualizations that inherit from `VisualizationElement`.

The reason it is needed for the `CanvasGrid3D` class is that ThreeJS, a WebGL package used to render the objects in a WebGL `canvas` tag, must be loaded before the corresponding mouse controls package can be loaded.

The `timeout` is implemented with OS functions, depending on the platform, e.g. with `select`'s  timeout parameter and not with settings of the driver.

In contrast to `inter_byte_timeout` which is a setting of the driver and if that is working properly also depends on the driver and the capabilities of the chip (e.g. USB serial converter, serial port emulations). It would not surprise me if you find devices not doing that one properly.

- For 1st point: AllreduceParameter keeps unchanged which global distributed `parameters` still stores weight. The detailed synchronous EASGD update rule is as follows:

- For 2nd point: Each executor update local full-size model and update elastic difference to AllReduceParameter synchronous, so each executor's model differs from each other as the training goes. Here is the selection process that we went through, as I remember it. I'll say up front that this wasn't an exhaustive search, but there was some rationale behind it:

* **SMARTS** widely used in the solar industry, and well validated. Downside is that it does not model clouds
* **MODTRAN** very heavily validated, but not open source. This is the tool against which all other tools are measured. The libraries that @NelisW uses looks like they do a great job of reading in the outputs of MODTRANS, but it isn't likely to get integrated fully into a workflow
* **SBDART** this tool has the added advantage of being able to model various forms of clouds, and is validated against MODTRAN <http://www.arm.gov/publications/tech_reports/handbooks/rss/publications/ASES2006_Gueymard.pdf>

* **RRTM** we evaluated this, it seemed to be a very versatile model, however it seemed like it might be too skewed towards atmospheric science and so might be a bit bulky/hard to use. That being said, that's not something that a nice clean wrapper can't fix. 

The license for SBDART is a bit ambiguous, the code  is available at a few locations online, but I'm not sure what the actual license is for the code. The choice of SMARTS and SBDART was made because they seemed to be a good balance between utility, size, accuracy and liscencing. 

As for the liscence of PYRTM, the creators are myself and @uniphil, and I believe we would be open to modifying the terms to whatever makes the code most useful.

As I have learnt in the meantime (see https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/478), the tactic 

`(par-then partition smt)`

would not only execute the second tactic in parallel, but would also stop when the first sat were found by one of the SMT-solvers. Instead

`(check-sat-using (then partition smt))`

would have to be used. However, that statement, elegant as it is, would unfortunately not benefit from concurrency.

I traced down this bug to libffi. In summary, newer gcc versions assume a 16-byte aligned stack, while libffi only guarantees a 4-byte aligned stack (on x86). Hence the crashes.
The issue is already documented in, e.g., http://bugs.python.org/issue17423
There's a work around, though, which would be to mark all API functions as potentially misaligned on 32-bit gcc platforms, but I guess it's not worth the effort.  Compiling with -O2, or disabling compilation with SSE "solves" the issue.

There is no correct or incorrect here. Different people will copy the files into different directories, sometimes manually, sometimes via package managers. It's fine to add a missing path if it helps you, but it would only create unnecessary trouble to remove paths. 

Users copy files around manually, they don't use CMake to build or to install, so we have to have a way to let them tell us exactly where they expect the library. In the past that was done via `init(...)`, but I removed that because it created a bunch of trouble with the garbage collector (_lib being collected at the wrong time), plus I'm not aware of any users re-initializing via `init(...)`. Most recent example of usage of `init(...)` is #1348, where the user wants to use libraries from a binary/nightly release without building or installing anything.

In general, mk_api_doc.py does not need to be in the CI runs. It's used one per year by myself, and everybody else goes to the webpage. So, I'd rather dirty-hack it instead of spending hours to fix it. 

If you have time to spend on this, feel free to propose a solution that fixes all of this without breaking any existing or future installations, but it will be a rough ride with lots of bug requests to answer soon after it gets merged...

> btw, as you stated: ['output'] return other layer, but I cannot find place where you define getitem for CellLayer, what is the purpose of calling it before you add cell to RecurrentContainer?

You don't have to, but then you get both the cell and output states. The `__getitem__` makes sure you only get the output state. It is not specific to cells, and is [implemented here](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/629/files#diff-1aca1664756f89ab20b6716803219df1R260).

The alternative would be adding the batch normalization training Op directly to Theano, and adding an optimization that replaces it with the cuDNN version if possible. Spaces have a specific meaning in Markdown URLs both in CommonMark and the [original Markdown "spec"](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/syntax#link):

> To create an inline link, use a set of regular parentheses immediately after the link text’s closing square bracket. Inside the parentheses, put the URL where you want the link to point, along with an optional title for the link, surrounded in quotes. For example:

> `This is [an example](http://example.com/ "Title") inline link.`

> `[This link](http://example.net/) has no title attribute.`

A URL with spaces is not a valid URL. The URL with `%20` is URL encoded, quite valid. There is an important reason to support the title attribute - for accessibility and screen readers. This becomes more important when doing links on images from Markdown.

> `kinto.event_listeners.redis.resources = record`
> `kinto.event_listeners.redis.resourceId = mybucket mycollection` 

One detail: so far we have used underscores instead of camelcase, we should stick to it ;)
And it should be plural since we can specify a list.

In your example, you filter by `record` but specify id values that look like buckets and collections. I suppose you want record events but only from specific bucket/collection.

It makes me think of something. Suppose we have the following:
```ini
kinto.event_listeners.redis.resources = record
kinto.event_listeners.redis.object_ids = blocklist certificates
```
What happens if the event is triggered on `/buckets/certificates/collections/pinning/records` or `/buckets/settings/collections/blocklist/records`? It is kept obviously, but what about `/buckets/foo/collections/bar/records/blocklist-record-id/`?  Should we leave the ambiguity of which resource it refers to? Maybe, I don't know...  @Natim f?

We could still remove the ambiguity by prefixing the `_ids` settings with the resource type:
```ini
kinto.event_listeners.redis.resources = record
kinto.event_listeners.redis.bucket_ids = blocklist catalog
kinto.event_listeners.redis.collection_ids = certificates addons fennec
```
In this case, the only ambiguity that remains is about the possible combinations of bucket/collections: eg. both `blocklist/addons` and `catalog/addons` will be kept. Is the case that becomes a problem, we could still define several listeners like this:
```ini
kinto.event_listeners.redis_blocklist.resources = record
kinto.event_listeners.redis_blocklist.bucket_ids = blocklist
kinto.event_listeners.redis_blocklist.collection_ids = certificates addons

kinto.event_listeners.redis_catalog.resources = record
kinto.event_listeners.redis_catalog.bucket_ids = catalog
kinto.event_listeners.redis_catalog.collection_ids = fennec
```
I'm not saying you should follow my propositions, it's just a way to challenge the idea and come up with a solid plan :) 

What do you think?

Not adding an opinion, but it's important to consider the AWS service limits [1] and see if it's fit for purpose. 

Key limits affecting parameter store are:
   - 10,000 parameters per account
   - 4096 chars max per parameter 
   - 100 past values

So there's no official rate-limit on requests per second, compared to DynamoDB provisioned reads.

However, the 4096char parameter size (not sure if that's pre or post encryption) could be a pain if storing certificate chains or whole configuration files.

 [1] [AWS Service Limits for Systems Manager](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/aws_service_limits.html#limits_ssm)

I guess you need some logic which waits until a new frame is ready so you can process it and save it. I think your program crash since the frame is not ready yet and you don't have a handler for that. Do it like this:

```
import argparse
import pyrealsense2 as rs
import numpy as np
import cv2
import os


def main():
    if not os.path.exists(args.directory):
        os.mkdir(args.directory)
    try:
        config = rs.config()
        rs.config.enable_device_from_file(config, args.input)
        pipeline = rs.pipeline()
        config.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 1280, 720, rs.format.z16, 30)
        pipeline.start(config)
        i = 0
        while True:
            print("Saving frame:", i)
            frames = rs.composite_frame(rs.frame())
            # Check if new frame is ready
            if pipeline.poll_for_frames(frames):
                depth_frame = frames.get_depth_frame()
                depth_image = np.asanyarray(depth_frame.get_data())
                cv2.imwrite(args.directory + "/" + str(i).zfill(6) + ".png", depth_image)
                i += 1
            # wait logic until frame is ready
            else:
                print("Waiting for frame to be ready")
    finally:
        pass


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("-d", "--directory", type=str, help="Path to save the images")
    parser.add_argument("-i", "--input", type=str, help="Bag file to read")
    args = parser.parse_args()
    main()
```

Here's my own sample script that I came up with today:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
"""Execute the "ping" module on all hosts in an inventory file."""
from ansible.executor.task_queue_manager import TaskQueueManager
from ansible.inventory import Inventory
from ansible.parsing.dataloader import DataLoader
from ansible.playbook.play import Play
from ansible.vars import VariableManager
from collections import namedtuple


Options = namedtuple('Options', [
    'become',
    'become_method',
    'become_user',
    'check',
    'connection',  # 'smart'
    'forks',  # 5
    'module_path',
    'private_key_file',
    'remote_user',
    'scp_extra_args',  # ''
    'sftp_extra_args',  # ''
    'ssh_common_args',  # ''
    'ssh_extra_args',  # ''
    'verbosity',  # 0
])


def main():
    """Run the "ping" module on all hosts."""
    loader = DataLoader()
    variable_manager = VariableManager()
    inventory = Inventory(loader=loader, variable_manager=variable_manager)
    variable_manager.set_inventory(inventory)
    play = Play().load(
        {
            'name': 'my test play',
            'tasks': [{'action': {'module': 'ping'}}],
        },
        loader=loader,
        variable_manager=variable_manager,
    )

    task_qm = None
    try:
        task_qm = TaskQueueManager(
            inventory=inventory,
            variable_manager=variable_manager,
            loader=loader,
            options=Options(
                None,  # become
                None,  # become_method
                None,  # become_user
                None,  # check
                'ssh',  # connection
                None,  # forks
                None,  # module_path
                None,  # private_key_file
                None,  # remote_user
                None,  # scp_extra_args
                None,  # sftp_extra_args
                None,  # ssh_common_args
                None,  # ssh_extra_args
                None,  # verbosity
            ),
            passwords=None,
        )
        task_qm.run(play)
    finally:
        if task_qm is not None:
            task_qm.cleanup()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

I personally think this one should be "extensible". For instance, I'd rather start with something like:

```
position: position of the ace in the ACL
protocol: ip, ipv6, arp, etc...
protocol_options: if needed, TBD as needed
source: whatever the source may be
source_options: if needed, TBD as needed
source_interface:
destination: whatever the destination may be
destination_options: if needed, TBD as needed
destination_interface:
transport_protocol: l4 protocol
transport_options: if needed, TBD as needed
```

So, for instance, TCP would have as transport_options source_port, destination_port, flags (SYN, ACK), established... UDP would have only source and destination ports, ICMP would have type and code and other protocols may have other options or just a subset of them.

For QoS we should do something similar but I refuse to spend brain cycles thinking about anything related to QoS so I would leave it to other people :P

An alternative is to have different data structures depending on the ACE and discuss them as needed. For instance:

```
---
type: tcp
source:
source_port:
destination:
destination_port:
is_established:
flags
---
type: udp
source:
source_port:
destination:
destination_port:
---
type: icmp
source:
destination:
type:
code:
```

In any case, if we do a flat structure this is going to grow into a million heads hydra that is going to be pretty much useless or unnecessarily complex to use (from a user perspective).

I don't use the feature, but I can think of two use cases where being able to change the redirector would be useful:

1. Your `cmd2` application allows the user to enter SQL statements, which often contain '>' as a greater than comparison operator. 
2. As the current documentation points out, if your `cmd2` application does lots of math work, then the '>' would be more likely used as a comparison operator than a redirection operator.

If we remove `Cmd2.redirector` you can still address these use cases by making your commands multi-line: the current parsing logic only treats '>' and '>>' as redirection tokens in multiline commands when they are preceded by the command terminator (usually ';'). The work-around may not be completely desirable because you might want your `cmd2` application to override the `default()` method to execute the SQL, and you can't make the default command be a multi-line command.

While we wait for others to chime in if they use this or not, the following areas of the codebase will need to be modified to fully support this attribute:

- `Cmd2.tokens_for_completion()`
- `Cmd2._redirect_complete()`
- `Cmd2._cmdloop()`
- `StatementParser` class

I've been tinkering with a solution for this using a curiously recurring mixin. WIP can be seen in  https://github.com/dean0x7d/pybind11/commit/3624708673658a2dc2ef59208890ea0650bc7020 and https://github.com/dean0x7d/pybind11/commit/088db874a9bdb2f415b2aab2bba6edcf167c1cbe. The basic idea is to extend `attr` and other accessors (item/tuple/list) with the `object_api` mixin. This approach extends the accessors without duplicating code and it preserves the current lazy evaluation of attributes/items.

Now, I ran into an issue here, which isn't actually related to the extended API but rather to the optional nature of `py::object`. The issue is that something like
```c++
obj.attr("value").attr("method")(1, 2, 3)
``` 
may return a `nullptr` at any point along the chain. This makes error handling and reporting very difficult. The existing implementation of `accessor` actually discards Python's original error message and just returns a `nullptr` in `py::object`. Because of this, the chained implementation requires additional `nullptr` checks in multiple places in `accessor`. The error messages also need to be recreated from scratch since the Python originals were discarded.

Adding lots of additional `nullptr` checking can work, but it's not exactly elegant. This brings me to a breaking change proposal. I know there are breaking changes in the pipeline for v2.0, but I'm not sure how soon this is planned to happen? In any case, here is the proposal.

#### Guarantee that `py::object` is never `nullptr`

Currently, any `py::object` (or derived) instance can have **two** invalid states: `nullptr` and `None`. This isn't ideal. While `None` is expected from Python and unavoidable, the `nullptr` state is not. `None` results in nice Python error messages, while `nullptr` results in anything from an internal Python C API error to undefined behavior. Removing the latter from user-facing code would be very beneficial.

A further compilation is that it's currently not very clear when a `nullptr` should be checked for. Not everything will return `nullptr` for an error: for example `py::make_tuple` will throw an exception instead of `nullptr` and `py::cast` can actually do either. It's currently more common that a `nullptr` will be returned. This is fine for internal code and for `py::handle`, but it would be nice if `py::object` was guaranteed to be non-`nullptr` or throw an exception otherwise. This would make the behavior consistent both within the library and with Python (`AttributeError` with a clear error message, rather than a `nullptr`).

`py::object` and derived classes would then become completely safe end-user types. For internal use an optionally `nullptr` type is still required. This is what it would look like:

 * `py::handle` -- remains unchanged: May be `nullptr`. Manual reference counting.
 * `py::owned_handle` -- new type: May be `nullptr`. Automatic ref counting. Intended for implementation machinery and expert users (can leak ref count or dereference `nullptr` if user is not careful).
 * `py::object` -- becomes stricter: Guaranteed never to be `nullptr`. Automatic ref counting. Intended as a safe user type.

Everything else would still derive from `py::object`, nothing from `py::owned_handle`. I've done some preliminary work and it wouldn't be difficult to convert `py::object` -> `py::owned_handle` where appropriate internally. The `py::object`s at interfaces would remain there -- they would just become safer.

The change is breaking, but only in one point: `py::object::operator bool()` will no longer be there. Overall, I think the change would be very much worth it and I can do the work if this seems reasonable.

Thanks @I8Robot. If I've understood correctly, pybind does not allow an interpreter to persist when a function it was created in exits and at the same time, if I try to recreate it, loading external modules will fail because the underlying CPython could not deallocate them properly. 

Thus it seems that if I want to call an embedded interpreter to do some tasks using e.g. numpy several times during program lifetime I cannot do it with pybind. The other option available through the low-lefel CPython API is creating sub-interpreters (https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/init.html#c.Py_NewInterpreter) or using CPython's API to keep the interpreter alive for the entire program duration.

I think there is a problematic aspect of this, which might lead to hard-to-find bugs. Imagine the following contrived piece of code which should probably fail:
```
b = a.get_const_reference()
blackbox()
b.mutate()
```

However, if``blackbox()`` acquires a non-const reference, the ``b.mutate()`` call will succeed. Due to the global connection of const and non-const references, a later change in ``blackbox()`` may trigger this fault. At that point, it may be very hard to understand why something that previously worked now doesn't.

This is going to be a wontfix wrt the default locations deployed by Certbot.  According to the [Filesystem Hierarchy Standard](http://refspecs.linuxfoundation.org/FHS_3.0/fhs/ch03s07.html), `/etc/` is used for the following purpose:

> The /etc hierarchy contains configuration files. A "configuration file" is a local file used to control the operation of a program; it must be static and cannot be an executable binary.

Now, you can perhaps argue that certificates are not used to control the operation of programs, but in many important ways they do. Furthermore, existing distributions have made the same choice: Debian puts its native certs in `/etc/ssl/` and Fedora puts them in `/etc/pki`. We'd understand if those OSes customized Certbot to deploy to their historical cert storage locations, but we may alternatively try to polish the `/etc/letsencrypt/live` / `/etc/letsencrypt/archive` arrangement and turn it into a standard, because it has a number of advantages over the way things were done previously.

Despite all of this, if you'd like your certs to go somewhere different, you have a few options:

1. Move all of `/etc/letsencrypt`, which you can do with the `--config-dir` flag
2. Use a `--renew-hook` to deploy certs and keys out of `/etc/letsencrypt/live` to wherever you want them (we recommend copying rather than moving)
3. Certbot 0.10.0 is going to support a lot more customisation of cert and key locations via editing of the renewal configuration file (in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/`) for each cert.
If you cert is due for renewal and a hook fails, it will print the error to `stderr` but still try to renew the cert. A failing hook doesn't cause Certbot to exit with a non-zero error code.

In investigating #3552, I found that #3533 does not fix this issue. In #3533, we added a call to `has_pending_deployment` to `_handle_identical_cert_request`. However, `_handle_identical_cert_request` is only ever called from `_treat_as_renewal`, which is only ever called from `_auth_from_domains` where we skip the call to `_treat_as_renewal` if the function was called with a `lineage` parameter. Since we always call this function with a `lineage` parameter during renewal, the change in #3533 has no effect on the `renew` subcommand.

Unfortunately, the core Certbot team had a design meeting recently where we decided to deprecate and remove support for experimental platforms in `certbot-auto` where there are better options available such as Homebrew or MacPorts (see #4520). `certbot-auto` was simply meant to be a temporary solution until Certbot was properly packaged. This work has certainly be done on macOS and we don't want `certbot-auto` used on the platform because it is much less stable than other options.

We're adding the deprecation messages this release and won't be accepting any additional changes until support is ultimately removed. If things worked for people, great, but we're actively discouraging people from using the platform and don't want to make/maintain any changes that may cause issues for existing users during the transition period. I'm a little confused unless they can provide a little more comprehensive a reproduction process, to be honest.

All formulae in Homebrew that use Python resources _(are supposed to)_ install those resources into `libexec/vendor` under [the old](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/blob/e4f74167c8f13f775a0f74735c48c603f1b3fa12/Formula/certbot.rb#L20-L182) vendoring system, and just `libexec` under [our newer virtualenv-based](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/blob/d9f460671a4c87ae9e28120227fe3bb706b60c40/Formula/certbot.rb#L21-L166) system. As far as I'm aware we're missing no resources there that would cause a fallback to a globally user-installed one if available.

Those resources don't live outside the keg, aren't "publicly" available _(by design)_, and install in the same way regardless of whether using Homebrew's Python or the system Python. Changing a shebang shouldn't fix anything about the way `certbot` runs in Homebrew, unless you've done something like fun like remove `/usr/bin/python`.

I'm not really sure what to recommend unless OP can provide a bit more information.

Although I am creating this issue, I note my dissent here, because consider two cases in how we deployed the cert. First, we previously installed a cert on a plaintext-ish block. In this case, that block already has a plaintextish listen directive on a non-443 port, and we would have found that block and added the redirect statement to it. Thus, we only encounter this problem in the second case, where we previously installed a cert on a sslish block that doesn't also have a plaintextish listen directive in it. This means that before we came along the user didn't get that http traffic at all, and now they do and it redirects to https.

I don't think we should make this change. Suddenly changing this will break things for existing users when they pull in a new version of the Docker image. I also think the goal of the Docker image is to run Certbot so let's continue to do that by default. I don't think most people need to make internal changes to their container and most changes come externally from mounting different paths, exposing different ports, etc. You can change the entrypoint yourself by adding `--entrypoint=sh` on the command line.

I'd also encourage you to look into automating solving DNS challenges as having to do it every 2-3 months is obviously a bit of a pain. We have some [DNS plugins](https://pypi.python.org/pypi?%3Aaction=search&term=certbot&submit=search) that may be able to help with this if you're using a popular provider, but otherwise, it might be useful to look into [writing a script yourself](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#hooks) to automate this if possible.

As for the volumes, [we recommend](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/install.html#running-with-docker) that people mount the volumes on their host. I'm sorry to hear this doesn't work well on Windows. We specify volumes for these directories in our Dockerfile so files such as your private key or certificates aren't lost by default when you shut down the container.

If there is a sane way for us to determine the path of the volume on the host, we could potentially land a change to correct Certbot output, however, then output is incorrect when inside the container (unless you've mounted the volumes to the same path on your host). I think this is a general problem with paths inside of a Docker container or other virtualization and I've never seen a good solution here.

I'm closing this issue since there is the `--entrypoint` workaround available to you that doesn't affect existing users, but if there are other problems that you think I'm missing, please let me know.

Hi, sorry to interrupt, but would it be at all possible to avoid having the DataSet having a hard dependency on sqlite? This seems to violate the valued principle of loose coupling.

I'll have to look into it in a bit more detail (only stumbled upon this PR now), but instead of hard-coding  calls to imported non-instance functions in the DataSet object, wouldn't it make more sense to use the dependency-injection design pattern? Ie, give the constructor an object that is responsible for the persistence of the DataSet, instead of relying on non-object methods imported from the sqlite_base module. So basically, have an object that represents the sqlite3 db, and define methods on that object that are now functions in the sqlite_base module.

That way, the whole DataSet class becomes more future-proof and allows for customisation by simply giving the constructor another object with the same interface but for example writing to mongodb, memory, hdf5 or whatever. Doing things this way breaks the dependency of DataSet on sqlite_base, which makes the code more maintainable and testable, and thus more future-proof and stable.

The same I think can be said for Experiment class.

1 and 3 are similar. 1 would mean having something like `DOCTR_DEPLOY_ENCRYPTION_KEY_DRDOCTR_DOCTR` and `DOCTR_DEPLOY_ENCRYPTION_KEY_DRDOCTR_DRDOCTR_GITHUB_IO` and 3 would mean having `DOCTR_DEPLOY_KEY_DRDOCTR_DOCTR` and `DOCTR_DEPLOY_KEY_DRDOCTR_DRDOCTR_GITHUB_IO`. In either case we have to figure out some way to put the repo name in the environment variable name (maybe we should just hash it). The difference is that 1 would continue to use the Fernet encryption to encrypt the deploy ssh key and store the encryption key in the environment variable, and 3 would do away with the Fernet encryption and storing of the ssh key in the file and just store the ssh key in the environment variable ("stored in the environment variable" here means "encrypted with the Travis public key and then stored as a secure env var", obviously).

The Fernet encryption is a level of indirection that seems to be unnecessary (I didn't realize at the time that the private ssh key would fit in an environment variable by itself). It also isn't really more secure because the encrypted private ssh key file is publicly available, just sitting there in the git repo, so if the Fernet encryption key (the contents of the `DOCTR_DEPLOY_ENCRYPTION_KEY` environment variable) were leaked, it would be no different than if the private ssh key itself were leaked. Maybe it's marginally better for a private repo (I can't imagine how you would be able to access a leaked Travis.com environment variable and not have access to the repo, but maybe it could happen). 

The indirection also makes things quite confusing as there's three secret encryption keys, the Travis public/private key, the GitHub deploy public/private key, and the Fernet key, when really you only need the first two. The Fernet encryption only seems useful if you need to encrypt something that is too large to fit in an environment variable, and must be stored in a file. I think a deploy ssh key doesn't fit this bill.

The problem was that some earlier experiments had caused the local scheduler on one of the machines to enter a state in which it repeatedly retried certain redis commands. 

This caused the file `/tmp/raylogs/local_scheduler_0-083115.err` to fill up with lines like

```
[WARN] (/home/ubuntu/ray/src/common/state/table.cc:85) retrying operation local_scheduler_table_send_info, retry_count = -1
```

and ultimately to take up several gigabytes of disk.

So there wasn't any disk left on the machine, and that caused commands like

```python
import tempfile
tempfile.gettempdir()
```

to fail on that machine.

Removing the excessively large logfiles solved the problem. This is related to #526.

> if this is modified for multiple plugins

Depends on what will get changed. There are a couple of error messages with "redundant" words, so if that's the motivation behind this, then a lot of other messages also have to be rewritten.

I personally think that changing log output is bad if you also don't do a major version bump, as it will simply break dependencies. There is no other way than reading and parsing log output if you're building an application around Streamlink which is not written in Python or does have proper bindings or other IPC methods.

So if you want to change already "established" log messages (which were already included in the latest stable release), the entire log output should at least be tagged with unique codes based on the class/plugin, so that other applications can reliably parse it. Currently, only the log channel, its origin and message is being logged. Changing it would still require a major version bump, but since these changes are so minor and IMO irrelevant, I don't think they should be done now and we should instead wait until Streamlink is ready for a proper 1.0.0. Maybe #1690 will make this a bit easier to change.

I don't know whether anyone on the team uses QGIS or not, and I haven't used it for any serious projects. In any case, this question is outside of our scope, but I've attempted to put together a brief, Googled answer anyway. 

Laspy is a python library, so it is installed in the usual way described in the documentation. It does look like QGIS provides a Python console and a package exposing its API, but it also [looks like this is not dependent on the system Python install](https://www.lutraconsulting.co.uk/blog/2016/03/02/installing-third-party-python-modules-in-qgis-windows/).  That link may be helpful in getting the package installed. 

I will also say that we certainly do not have any built-in support for the QGIS API, so once you get it installed it's up to you to figure out how to get QGIS to work with the data. [This link](https://gis.stackexchange.com/questions/19338/viewing-lidar-data-las-in-qgis) may be helpful in this regard, or in suggesting alternate tools.
That would be welcome!

Hi, thank you for pointing out this ambiguity. `address()` is meant to return an address for `scriptPubkey`s that support it (in our case, we consider only `P2PKH`, `P2SH`, `P2WPKH` and `P2WSH` to properly support an address). `to_address(segwit_version=None)` is instead implemented for any script type and is meant to produce a `P2(W)SH` address for the script.

I can see the semantic ambiguity, so instead of fixing this as proposed in #28, I'd suggest renaming the two methods to something more appropriate. Any suggestion?

```
obj = Foo()
```

where `do_stuff` is defined on `Foo`.

DOMWidget-like behavior is expected on a few scenarios like display in Jupyter, or placing the instance inside `VBox`/`HBox`, embedding the widget in static contexts etc.

@bddppq - It's possible to populate the map by using the same couple code lines as added to cpp2py_export.cc.  Is it your goal to make this change non-breaking, so that updates are not required in any callers?

Another downside to that change would be coupling the policy of whether to use OpSchemaRegistry with the policy of which operator sets and versions will be used, in which registry.  

This could also make it less clear whether other code in the ONNX library should rely on this map.  For a similar reason, I was considering removing OpSchemaRegistry's internal statics altogether, and making callers create their own static instances of OpSchemaRegistry and share them as needed.  This is more disruptive to calling code though.

I'd prefer to keep this simple and define operator sets directly for now.  Code generation could be a more involved change if designed holistically (considering the most efficient format to generate, how to best handle inferencing callbacks, etc.).   The iteration routines in the existing PR also provide more flexibility for the library consumer, without requiring them to use a code-gen step.

This is not the case anymore. Pixiedust has a built-in Mapbox token.

The issue comes from the fact that %pylab is defining the "display" variable first before pixiedust does it. In this case, pixiedust will not override and leave the display variable defined as it is, causing the issue with the PixieDebugger PixieApp which is trying to use the display variable. I've provided a fix that detects this situation and uses the fully qualified "pixiedust.display" name instead.
Note: if you want to use the pixiedust display function, you'll need to fully qualify as well.

The old (no, not normal) on_recv methods gave no information, so the new descriptive name is only informative if you already understand on_recv.  The reason why I dislike adding a qualifier to the name is that the new methods are meant to *replace* the old ones, as a year and a half of writing apps with them has proven to me that this is how they should have been written in the first place. The only reason they are separate is so that I don't break existing apps while users upgrade.  That is less likely to take hold if the new name suggests that it is a bonus version, rather than the replacement that it is.

That said, maybe the best approach is to let it be, and use a more descriptive name, leaving the original bad decision in forever.
